column1: Major Characters and Gods of Zoroastrianism

Below is a table summarizing key Zoroastrian deities and legendary figures, with notes on their first citations and major appearances in Zoroastrian texts and tradition. This list focuses on gods and divine beings, not historical or priestly figures, and draws on the Avesta, later Pahlavi texts, and key secondary sources234578.

column1: First citations generally refer to the earliest surviving Zoroastrian texts, especially the Gathas and Yashts of the Avesta. Later Pahlavi texts (e.g., Bundahishn, Denkard) elaborate on these figures.
Amesha Spentas are the seven "Holy Immortals"-aspects or emanations of Ahura Mazda, each with a cosmic and ethical domain2345.
Yazatas are lesser divinities or angels, often personifying natural forces or moral qualities3578.
Daevas are the class of rejected gods or demons, originally Indo-Iranian deities reinterpreted as evil by Zoroaster27.
Saoshyant is a future messianic figure, first mentioned in later parts of the Avesta and developed in post-Avestan literature12.

This table covers the principal divine and mythic beings in Zoroastrian cosmology as attested in the earliest and most authoritative sources. For a more exhaustive list, including minor Yazatas, legendary kings, and demons, further consultation of the Avesta, Bundahishn, and academic encyclopedias is recommended.
